question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user open an unbounded number of TCP connections to the gRPC port (since SpringApplication.run() imposes no global connection cap) to exhaust server-side file descriptors, causing the gRPC server to reject all new connections and halt transaction confirmation?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: grpcServerConfigurer()] Can an unprivileged external user open many simultaneous TCP connections (each allowed maxConcurrentCallsPerConnection=5 calls) to multiply the effective call count beyond what the applicationTaskExecutor thread pool can handle, starving all worker threads and preventing any new transaction-related gRPC calls from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: subscribeTopic()] Can an unprivileged external user send a ConsensusTopicQuery with limit=0 (hasLimit() returns false) and no endTime to create a permanent never-completing subscription that holds a boundedElastic scheduler thread indefinitely, and by repeating this across many connections exhaust all scheduler threads causing total service unavailability?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/GrpcApplication.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a high-traffic topic via SharedTopicListener.listen() and deliberately slow down consumption so that the onBackpressureBuffer(maxBufferSize, BufferOverflowStrategy.ERROR) fills up, causing an overflow error that terminates the shared Flux and disrupts all other subscribers on the same shared listener?
